stem: The waiting time (in minutes) until the next customer arrives is exponentially distributed with rate \( \lambda = [[lam]] \) per minute. What is the probability that the next customer arrives within [[t]] minutes (that is, the waiting time is at most [[t]])?
solution: For an exponential variable the CDF is \( P(T \le t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t} = 1 - e^{-[[lam]] \cdot [[t]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( P(T > [[t]]) = e^{-\lambda t} \), the survival probability (complement).
This uses the mean \( 1/\lambda \) in place of the rate \( \lambda \) in the exponent.
This omits the time [[t]] from the exponent.
This omits the rate \( \lambda \) from the exponent.
This is the survival probability with the rate and mean confused.
This uses a linear approximation \( 1 - \lambda t \) instead of \( 1 - e^{-\lambda t} \).

stem: The lifetime (in years) of a certain electronic component is exponentially distributed with rate \( \lambda = [[lam]] \) per year. What is the probability that the component lasts MORE than [[t]] years?
solution: The survival function of an exponential variable is \( P(T > t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-[[lam]] \cdot [[t]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( P(T \le [[t]]) \), the CDF, the complement of the requested survival probability.
This uses the mean \( 1/\lambda \) in place of the rate \( \lambda \) in the exponent.
This is the CDF with the rate and mean confused.
This omits the time [[t]] from the exponent.
This omits the rate \( \lambda \) from the exponent.
This uses a linear expression instead of the exponential survival function.

stem: The time (in minutes) until a machine breaks down is exponentially distributed with rate \( \lambda = [[lam]] \) per minute. Given that the machine has already run for [[s]] minutes without breaking down, what is the probability that it runs for at least an additional [[t]] minutes (that is, a total of at least \( [[s]] + [[t]] \) minutes) before breaking down?
solution: By the memoryless property of the exponential distribution, \( P(T > s+t \mid T > s) = P(T > t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-[[lam]] \cdot [[t]]} = [[answer]] \); the elapsed time [[s]] does not matter.
distractor_rationales: This incorrectly treats the total elapsed time \( s+t \) as the relevant exponent, ignoring memorylessness.
This reports the probability of surviving only the ALREADY-elapsed time \( s \), not the additional wait.
This is the complement (probability of breaking down within the additional [[t]] minutes).
This double-counts by multiplying survival probabilities for [[s]] and [[t]] separately instead of using memorylessness.
This algebraically simplifies to \( e^{-\lambda s} \), which reintroduces the elapsed time that memorylessness should cancel.
This treats the waiting time as if it were uniform rather than exponential.

stem: The time (in minutes) between consecutive earthquakes at a fault line is exponentially distributed with rate \( \lambda = [[lam]] \) per minute. What is the probability that the time until the next earthquake is between [[a]] and [[b]] minutes?
solution: Use the survival function at each endpoint: \( P([[a]] \le T \le [[b]]) = P(T > [[a]]) - P(T > [[b]]) = e^{-\lambda [[a]]} - e^{-\lambda [[b]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This has the two survival values subtracted in the wrong order, giving a negative quantity.
This is \( P(T > [[a]]) \) alone, ignoring the upper endpoint [[b]].
This is \( P(T > [[b]]) \) alone, ignoring the lower endpoint [[a]].
This treats the exponent as the length of the interval rather than using the CDF at each endpoint.
This is the survival probability over the interval LENGTH, not over the actual endpoints [[a]] and [[b]].
This double-subtracts instead of taking the difference of the two survival values.

stem: The waiting time (in minutes) for a bus is exponentially distributed with rate \( \lambda = [[lam]] \) per minute. If the probability that you wait more than \( t \) minutes is \( e^{-[[k]]} \), what is \( t \)?
solution: Since \( P(T > t) = e^{-\lambda t} \), setting \( e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-[[k]]} \) gives \( \lambda t = [[k]] \), so \( t = \dfrac{[[k]]}{\lambda} = [[answer]] \) minutes.
distractor_rationales: This inverts the ratio, dividing the rate by the exponent instead of the exponent by the rate.
This multiplies \( \lambda \) and [[k]] instead of dividing.
This takes the reciprocal of the product instead of the quotient \( [[k]]/\lambda \).
This subtracts the rate from the exponent instead of dividing.
This is off by one from the correct value of \( t \).
This is off by one from the correct value of \( t \).
